title: The name on the label
summary: A Kenyan grandmother visiting London tells a museum guard the story of one small chair behind glass. She does not want it back.

body.speaker: Mrs Otieno
body.text: In the Kenyan room there is a small wooden chair. The label says 'Tribal chair, late 19th century.'

body.speaker: Mrs Otieno
body.text: She was eighteen. She made it for her first child. The chair was in my mother's house. My mother gave it to a British man in 1962 for one hundred shillings. He sold it. The chair travelled. Now it is here.

body.speaker: Mr Brown
body.text: Madam, can I tell my supervisor?

body.speaker: Mrs Otieno
body.text: Yes. But not today. Today, I just wanted somebody who works here to know.

body.speaker: Mrs Otieno
body.text: Write the name. Wanjiku. She lived from 1881 to 1956. She made many chairs. This one is the one I saw in my mother's house when I was a child.

body.speaker: Mrs Otieno
body.text: Thank you. I do not want the chair. I want the name on the label.

body.speaker: Mr Brown
body.text: I will say so to my supervisor.

annotations.meaningVi: nhãn / biển chú thích (trong bảo tàng)
đã chạm khắc (bằng dao trên gỗ)
shilling (đơn vị tiền cũ ở Kenya)
thuộc bộ tộc (cách gọi cũ, thường có vấn đề)

grammarNotes.title: Past simple — more irregular verbs in storytelling
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 tiếp tục mở rộng vốn động từ bất quy tắc. Trong bài: 'My grandmother carved that chair.' (regular: carve → carved). 'She was eighteen.' (be → was). 'She made it.' (make → made). 'My mother gave it to a British man.' (give → gave). 'He sold it.' (sell → sold). 'She lived…' (live → lived). 'I saw in my mother's house.' (see → saw). Mỗi câu là một bước trong câu chuyện một chiếc ghế.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 keeps growing the irregular-verb stock. The dialogue: 'My grandmother carved that chair.' (regular). 'She was eighteen.' (be → was). 'She made it.' (make → made). 'My mother gave it to a British man.' (give → gave). 'He sold it.' (sell → sold). 'I saw in my mother's house.' (see → saw). Each is one step in the chair's story.

translationVi:
Bà Otieno: Xin lỗi, ngài.
Ông Brown: Vâng, thưa bà?
Bà Otieno: Trong phòng Kenya có một cái ghế gỗ nhỏ. Nhãn ghi 'Ghế bộ tộc, cuối thế kỷ 19.'
Ông Brown: Tôi biết cái ghế ấy.
Bà Otieno: Bà tôi đã đẽo cái ghế ấy.
Ông Brown: … Thưa bà.
Bà Otieno: Bà mười tám tuổi. Bà làm cho đứa con đầu lòng. Cái ghế ở trong nhà mẹ tôi. Mẹ tôi đưa cho một người đàn ông Anh năm 1962, lấy một trăm shilling. Ông ấy bán đi. Cái ghế đi xa. Giờ nó ở đây.
Ông Brown: Thưa bà, tôi báo cấp trên được không?
Bà Otieno: Được. Nhưng không phải hôm nay. Hôm nay, tôi chỉ muốn có ai đó làm việc ở đây biết.
Ông Brown: Tôi sẽ ghi lại.
Bà Otieno: Ghi tên. Wanjiku. Bà sống từ 1881 đến 1956. Bà làm nhiều ghế. Cái này là cái tôi thấy ở nhà mẹ tôi khi tôi còn bé.
Ông Brown: Wanjiku.
Bà Otieno: Cảm ơn. Tôi không muốn cái ghế. Tôi muốn cái tên trên nhãn.
Ông Brown: Tôi sẽ nói vậy với cấp trên.
Bà Otieno: Vậy là đủ cho hôm nay.

options: Near a museum's Kenyan room (Mr Brown is a guard there)
questions.explanation: The Kenyan room with the chair.
questions.hint: Look at where the chair is.

questions.prompt: What does the label currently say?

options: Mrs Otieno's grandmother, when she was eighteen, for her first child
questions.explanation: 'My grandmother carved that chair. She was eighteen. She made it for her first child.'

questions.prompt: How did the chair leave Kenya?
options: Mrs Otieno's mother gave it to a British man in 1962 for one hundred shillings; he sold it.
It was donated by the Kenyan government.
It was found in a forest.

questions.prompt: What does Mrs Otieno NOT want?
options: Mr Brown to write down the name
questions.hint: Mrs Otieno states what she does NOT want.

questions.explanation: 'I want the name on the label.'

questions.prompt: Will Mr Brown tell his supervisor?
options: Yes — he says 'I will say so to my supervisor.'
questions.hint: Mr Brown's reply near the end.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A small change — adding one name to a museum label — can be a complete request, even when bigger things (the object itself) cannot easily be returned.
Museums should give all objects back.
questions.explanation: Mrs Otieno asks for naming, not restitution.
questions.hint: Notice the difference between the chair and the name.

cloze.template:
Mrs Otieno: Excuse me, sir.
Mr Brown: Yes, madam?
Mrs Otieno: In the Kenyan room there is a small wooden {{b1}}. The label says 'Tribal chair, late 19th century.'
Mr Brown: I know the chair.
Mrs Otieno: My grandmother {{b2}} that chair.
Mr Brown: … Madam.
Mrs Otieno: She was eighteen. She made it for her first child. The chair was in my mother's house. My mother {{b3}} it to a British man in {{b4}} for one hundred shillings. He sold it. The chair travelled. Now it is here.
Mr Brown: Madam, can I tell my {{b5}}?
Mrs Otieno: Yes. But not today. Today, I just wanted somebody who works here to know.
Mr Brown: I will {{b6}} it down.
Mrs Otieno: Write the name. {{b7}}. She lived from 1881 to 1956. She made many chairs. This one is the one I saw in my mother's house when I was a child.
Mr Brown: Wanjiku.
Mrs Otieno: Thank you. I do not want the {{b8}}. I want the {{b9}} on the label.
Mr Brown: I will say so to my supervisor.
Mrs Otieno: That is {{b10}} for today.

blanks.explanation: Gave it to a British man.
She does not want the chair.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mrs Otieno does not ask for the chair back — only for her grandmother's name on the label. Is there a place in your own life where a small acknowledgement would matter more than getting the larger thing returned? What would the new label say?
